coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic efficiency in C3N4 via particle and surface engineering of Graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Youliang
Bao, Rui
Xiao, Xiudi
Yi, Jianhong
Xu, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been employed to enhance the photocatalytic performance of carbon nitride (C3N4), leveraging their superior physicochemical characteristics. The photovoltaic attributes of GQDs, influenced by their particle sizes and surface states, are contingent upon their synthesis methods. This research focused on the synthesis of GQDs with diverse particle sizes and surface features by modifying carbon nanotubes (CNTs) using HNO3. The findings indicate that prolonging the reaction time decreases the size of GQD particles and augments the presence of surface functional groups. Upon forming heterojunctions with C3N4, the GQDs-96/HC3N4 composite showcased superior photocatalytic degradation performance towards tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and methyl orange (MO). This enhancement is attributed to the improved dispersibility and oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs-96, facilitating effective interaction with HC3N4. This research provides a new guideline and idea for regulating the particle size and surface state of GQDs for their application in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of C3N4 for the degradation of water pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDS)
Particle size
Surface modification
GQDS/H-C3N4
Electrons and holes
photocatalytic efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) via oxidative cleavage of CNTs
Heterojunction catalyst morphology and structure analysis

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Aqueous dispersions of CNTs over reactive time. b) CNT and GQD powders.
TEM and HR-TEM images of CNTs and GQDs at various stages. a) CNTs. b) GQDs-24. c) GQDs-96. d) GQDs-48. e) GQDs-72. f) GQDs-96. (insets: particle size distribution).
Comparative a) XRD Patterns and b) FT-IR Spectra of CNTs and GQDs.
High-Resolution a) C1 s and b) O1 s XPS Spectra for CNTs and GQDs.
SEM images of a) C3N4 and b) GQDs-96/HC3N4; c) TEM and d) HR-TEM of GQDs-96/HC3N4.
a) XRD pattern, b) FT-IR spectrum, c) XPS survey spectrum, d) C1 s, e) N1 s, and f) O1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of catalysts.

a) MO and d) TC degradation curve of catalysts under visible light irradiation. b) MO and e) TC degradation for linear fittings of pseudo-first-order kinetics model. c) MO and f) TC degradation efficiency of catalysts.

a) UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra and b) Tauc plots of catalysts. c) XPS valence band spectra and d) band structure diagram of C3N4 and GQDs-96/HC3N4. e) PL spectra and f) TPR spectra of catalysts.
a) Degradation efficiency of MO by GQDs-96/HC3N4 with different scavengers. ESR spectra of C3N4 and GQDs-96/HC3N4 for b) TEMPO-h +, c) DMPO-•O2 − and c) DMPO-•OH.
The mechanism schematic of enhanced photocatalytic performance of the GQDs/C3N4 heterojunction.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic efficiency in C3N4 via particle and surface engineering of Graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eState Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been employed to enhance the photocatalytic performance of carbon nitride (C3N4), leveraging their superior physicochemical characteristics. The photovoltaic attributes of GQDs, influenced by their particle sizes and surface states, are contingent upon their synthesis methods. This research focused on the synthesis of GQDs with diverse particle sizes and surface features by modifying carbon nanotubes (CNTs) using HNO3. The findings indicate that prolonging the reaction time decreases the size of GQD particles and augments the presence of surface functional groups. Upon forming heterojunctions with C3N4, the GQDs-96/HC3N4 composite showcased superior photocatalytic degradation performance towards tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and methyl orange (MO). This enhancement is attributed to the improved dispersibility and oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs-96, facilitating effective interaction with HC3N4. This research provides a new guideline and idea for regulating the particle size and surface state of GQDs for their application in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of C3N4 for the degradation of water pollutants.

GQDs with various particle sizes and surface characteristics (GQDs-24, GQDs-48, GQDs-72, GQDs-96) were prepared by tailoring the duration of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) exposure to HNO3. After building heterojunctions with C3N4, GQDs promoted light absorption and electron transfer to enhance the photocatalytic activity of C3N4. Among them, GQDs-96/HC3N4 exhibited the best photocatalytic degradation rates for tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and methyl orange (MO), owing to GQDs-96′s enhanced dispersibility and oxygen-functional groups, promoting effective bonding with HC3N4.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nitride (C3N4), a novel, metal-free semiconductor photocatalyst [1], has emerged as a prominent player in photocatalytic applications such as hydrogen evolution, toxic pollutants degradation, and renewable energy preparation from CO2 reduction [2] due to its suitable bandgap (∼2.7 eV) [3], visible light response [4], non-toxicity [5], simple preparation [6], low cost [7], and good physicochemical stability [8]. However, the unmodified C3N4 obtained directly from the thermal polymerization of melamine, dicyandiamide, and urea [9], exhibits limitations such as a limited specific surface area [10], weak absorption of visible light [11], and rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [12], significantly dampen its photocatalytic activity and restrict its application scope.

Morphology modulation to prepare nanostructures [13], elemental doping to modulate the energy band structure [14], and construction of heterojunction catalysts with another semiconductor [15] or carbon materials have been utilized to improve the photocatalytic performance of C3N4 [16]. Specifically, constructing heterojunction metal-free photocatalysts with carbon nanomaterials is an effective strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance of C3N4 due to the excellent electrical conductivity of carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes [17], graphene [18] and graphene oxide [19]. Nonetheless, the tendency of these materials to poorly disperse and aggregate due to strong van der Waals forces and limited surface functional groups poses challenges in forming stable interfacial bonds with C3N4 and achieving uniform dispersion [20], which results in limited enhancement of the photocatalytic performance and poor cycling stability.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the family of carbon-based nanomaterials [21], have attracted great interest due to their unique physicochemical and photovoltaic properties. CDs are defined as a zero-dimensional material consisting of a core of sp2/sp3 carbon skeleton and abundant surface functional groups [22], with lateral dimensions <20 nm, in a spherical shape [23–24]. Their electrical conductivity and exceptional dispersibility make CDs ideal for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of C3N4 [25–26]. Prior studies have shown CDs' ability to significantly boost the photocatalytic efficacy of carbon nitride. For instance, Liu et al. [27] demonstrated a threefold increase in diclofenac degradation efficiency with a CQD-modified ultrathin g-C3N4 photocatalyst, while Huang et al. [28] and Hu et al. [29] reported substantial enhancements in light absorption, charge transport, and hydrogen production rates with CD-integrated carbon nitride nanotubes and vesicles, respectively.

However, most research has focused on CDs in single structural forms, neglecting the impact of varying particle sizes and surface functionalities. This oversight hinders the development of CDs with optimal structures and properties for constructing more efficient heterojunction catalysts with C3N4. The properties of CDs are predominantly influenced by their synthesis methods, particle sizes, and surface states [30–36]. To date, the effect of CDs with different particle sizes and surface states on photocatalytic performance has not been thoroughly explored.

This study breaks new ground by using concentrated HNO3 to fragment carbon nanotubes (CNTs), producing a range of GQDs with distinct particle sizes and surface oxygen-containing functional groups through reaction time adjustment. Subsequently, these GQDs were combined with thermally polymerized C3N4 to form various GQDs/HC3N4 heterojunction catalysts. Comprehensive characterization and analysis reveal that GQDs-96, with the smallest particle size and highest concentration of oxygen-containing groups, significantly boosts the photocatalytic efficiency of C3N4.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs, Purity:>98wt%; Length:10–30μm; Diameter:20–30 nm) were sourced from Chengdu Organic Chemistry Co. Ltd. Concentrated HNO3 (64 %−68 %) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd. Chemicals (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd) including sodium hydroxide (NaOH), melamine, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), methyl orange (MO), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), and isopropyl alcohol (IPQ) were all of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (resistivity > 18.5 MΩ·cm) was employed in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) via oxidative cleavage of CNTs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized from MWCNTs using concentrated HNO3. Specifically, 500 mg of MWCNTs were combined with 250 mL of concentrated HNO3 in a round-bottom flask. The mixture was heated and stirred at 140 °C under reflux, leading to a color transition from black turbidity to brownish-red, yellow, and finally to white transparency with increasing heating duration. After cooling, excess HNO3 was removed via a rotary evaporator, and residual acid neutralized with NaOH. The resultant product was purified by dialysis for 3–5 days and lyophilized to obtain fluffy, flocculent GQDs. GQDs synthesized with heating durations of 24, 48, 72, and 96 h were designated as GQDs-24, GQDs-48, GQDs-72, and GQDs-96, respectively.

Melamine (20 g) was placed in a covered crucible and heated in a muffle furnace from room temperature to 550 °C at a rate of 2.1 °C/min, maintained for 4 h. The resultant bright yellow block was cooled, ground into a fine yellow powder, and labeled as C3N4.

A 20 mg quantity of GQDs was dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water with 2 g of C3N4 and stirred for 30 min for uniform dispersion. The mixture was then transferred to a sealed high-pressure reactor and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. Post-cooling, the product was centrifuged and dried to obtain the GQDs/HC3N4 heterojunction. Heterojunctions using GQDs prepared at different times were named accordingly, alongside controls HC3N4 and CNTs/HC3N4 for comparison.

The photocatalytic efficacy of the synthesized materials was evaluated by degrading TC and MO under visible light. For each assay, 100 mg of photocatalyst was suspended in 100 mL of MO (10 mg/L) or TC (15 mg/L) solution and pre-stirred in the dark for 30 min. Illumination was provided by a 300 W Xenon lamp with a > 400 nm filter. Samples were collected at 15-minute intervals, processed (centrifugation for MO, 0.22μm filtration for TC), and analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometry at 462 nm (MO) and 357 nm (TC) to determine concentration changes. For the reproducibility and reliability of the test result, each set of catalyst tests was repeated in triplicate.

The micro-morphology and particle size variations of the samples (CNTs, GQDs, and catalysts) were visualized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2-TF30, S-Twin) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Namo-450, FEI). The phases of samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) with Cu Kα radiation. The surface functional groups of the samples were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Bruker Tensor 27). The surface states, chemical compositions, and valence of the samples were discussed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Shimadzu/Kratos, Supra Axis+) with the C1 s peak (284.6 eV) as reference. Zeta potential analyzer (HORIBA SZ-100) was used to investigate the dispersion stability of samples dispersed in ultrapure water. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) was performed by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600i plus). Photo-luminescence (PL) spectrum was recorded from fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F7000). The transient photocurrent response (TPR) of catalysts was tested by electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of catalysts were detected using a Bruker model A300 spectrometer.

The sequential transformation of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) into graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through reaction with concentrated HNO3 is captured vividly in the observed color transition of the solution—from a turbid black to a transparent reddish-brown, then yellowish, and finally albescent—as depicted in Fig. 1a . This color progression reflects the chemical changes occurring during the reaction and is mirrored in the freeze-dried GQDs, which exhibit similar color transitions (Fig. 1b). Notably, while untreated CNT solutions show signs of precipitation and agglomeration over time (Fig. 1a, second image from the left), all GQDs samples maintain excellent dispersion without any signs of precipitation or agglomeration, indicating a successful synthesis of different GQDs.

TEM reveals the transformation from one-dimensional CNT structures to zero-dimensional GQDs (see Fig. 2 ). Initially, CNTs display a tube diameter range of 20–30 nm, extending laterally to the micrometer level with noticeable agglomeration (Fig.2a). Post-treatment with HNO3 results in GQDs of various sizes: GQDs-24 (Fig. 2b), GQDs-48 (Fig. 2d), GQDs-72 (Fig. 2e), and GQDs-96 (Fig. 2f) showing average particle sizes of 13.23 nm, 5.96 nm, 4.01 nm, and 2.84 nm, respectively.

The TEM image of GQDs-24 highlights the presence of graphene nanofragments, suggesting a side product of the oxidative cutting process. This sample also displays a broader size distribution compared to the more uniform and narrowly distributed particle sizes of GQDs-48, GQDs-72, and GQDs-96. High-resolution TEM imagery of GQDs-96 (Fig. 2c) showcases the crystalline nature of these dots, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm that aligns with the graphene (100) diffraction plane, indicating high-quality crystallinity.

This comprehensive characterization underscores the effectiveness of the oxidative cutting method in producing GQDs with controlled particle sizes by adjusting the reaction duration between CNTs and HNO3. The observed differences in dispersion and particle size distribution among the GQDs samples highlight the effects of reaction time on the properties of the resulting quantum dots.

The XRD patterns of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) provide insight into the structural changes resulting from oxidative treatment (Fig. 3a ). The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26° and 43°, characteristic of the graphitic carbon structure's (002) and (100) planes are evident in CNTs [35]. Post-treatment, the peak near 2θ=43° vanishes, and a singular broad peak around 2θ=28° emerges in all GQDs, indicative of the graphene (002) plane. This broadening and diminished peak intensity signals the disruption of the CNT tubular structure, leading to smaller, graphene-like GQDs.

FT-IR spectroscopy reveals distinct features between CNTs and GQDs (Fig. 3b). CNTs exhibit limited absorption peaks, indicating a sparse presence of oxygen functional groups. Conversely, GQDs display peaks corresponding to various bonds: in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrations of CH at 1425 and 870 cm−1, and stretching vibrations of -OH, C = O, C = C, and CO at 3470, 1720, 1610, and 1230 cm−1, respectively. Notably, the oxygen-containing groups (C = O) on GQDs surfaces increase with reaction time, reaching a peak at GQDs-96, suggesting an enhanced presence of carbonyl and carboxyl groups [36].

All the GQDs have a weak broad peak near 535.6 eV, which is related to the trace Na ions remaining from the acid-base neutralization reaction during the preparation of GQDs. The full XPS spectra (Fig. S1) further demonstrated the increase in their oxygen content in GQDs with extended reaction time.

The XPS high-resolution spectra further reveal the changes in the surface chemical composition and bonding structure of CNTs and GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4a , the C1 s spectra of CNTs can be deconvoluted to three peaks at 284.8, 285.1, and 287.0 eV, corresponding to C = C, CC, and CO, respectively, whereas the C1 s spectra of GQDs could be fitted to three peaks near 284.8, 286.4, and 288.5 eV, assigning to C = C/CC, CO, and C = O, respectively. the percentage of peak area of CO gradually decreases while the C = O gradually increases as the reaction time extends suggesting a conversion of CO to C = O under the influence of concentrated HNO3 at elevated temperatures [37]. The O1 s spectra provide further evidence of this transformation.

As shown in Fig. 4b, the O1 s spectra of CNTs could be fitted to three peaks located near 530.4, 532.3, and 533.0 eV, ascribed to C = O, CO, and COH, respectively, while the O1 s spectra of GQDs could be mainly fitted to two peaks located at 531.2 and 532.5 eV, correlating to C = O and CO, respectively. Similarly, the percentage of the peak area of C = O gradually increased with the lengthening of the reaction time and reached the maximum at GQDs-96, a result that consisted with the C1 s spectra.

Fig. S3 depicts in detail the principle of the process of preparing GQDs from CNTs. The refined particle size and increased presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, particularly carbonyl and carboxyl groups, significantly enhance the dispersibility (as evidenced by zeta potential measurements) of GQDs (see Fig. S2). This improved dispersibility promotes more uniform dispersion rather than agglomeration when integrating GQDs with C3N4 to construct heterojunctions. Moreover, the abundance of C = O groups on GQDs' surfaces facilitates stronger interfacial interactions and the formation of robust bonds with the amino amides on C3N4 surfaces. These attributes are crucial for developing efficient heterojunction catalysts with C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heterojunction catalyst morphology and structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM analyses provide insight into the morphological and structural changes induced by the integration of GQDs with C3N4. Fig. 5a highlights the initial two-dimensional lamellar structure of C3N4, which limits its specific surface area and extends the migration paths for photogenerated electrons and holes, negatively impacting photocatalytic efficiency [38]. In contrast, the hydrothermally treated GQDs-96/HC3N4, depicted in Fig. 5b, exhibits a rod-like structure, significantly increasing active photocatalytic sites and, consequently, the efficiency of photocatalyst reactions [39].

Fig.5c demonstrates the uniform distribution of GQDs-96 over HC3N4, suggesting strong interfacial bonding due to GQDs-96′ s nanoscale size, superior dispersion, and abundant surface oxygen-containing functional groups, which effectively bond with HC3N4's amino groups. The average particle size of GQDs-96 is 2.91 nm, and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images (Fig. 5d) reveal a lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, consistent with previous analyses of GQDs-96. This uniform distribution and robust interfacial bonding are pivotal for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction.

XRD patterns of the catalysts (Fig. 6a ) show two typical characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 12.8° and 27.3° for both C3N4 and HC3N4, corresponding to the in-plane structural filling motifs of the tri-s-triazine unit (100) crystal face, and the latter attributable to the interlayer stacking of the graphite-like crystal structure (002) crystal face. Compared with C3N4 and HC3N4, the diffraction peak intensity of the (002) crystal face of GQDs-96/HC3N4 is significantly reduced and the peak position is shifted from 2θ = 27.3° to 2θ = 27.7°, indicating interlayer interactions and the prevention of C3N4 interlayer stacking due to GQDs-96 loading.

FT-IR results (Fig. 6b) highlight the presence of an additional weak peak near 1720 cm−1 in GQDs-96/HC3N4, attributed to C = O stretching vibrations, signifying GQDs-96 incorporation and interaction within the heterojunction. The absorption band at 808 cm−1 is attributed to the typical stretching vibration of the tri-s-triazine structural unit in C3N4 and the broad absorption bands at 2900–3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of NH in the incompletely condensed amino group and OH in the small amount of H2O molecules adsorbed on the surface of C3N4. Several strong absorption bands at 1240, 1321, 1409, 1538, and 1629 cm−1 (1200–1650 cm−1 region) correspond to the typical stretching vibrations of CN and C = N heterocycles.

XPS further elucidates the surface chemical composition and bonding states. The elevated oxygen content in GQDs-96/HC3N4, shown in Fig. 6c, is due to oxygen incorporation during hydrothermal synthesis and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs-96. C1 s spectra analysis (Fig. 6d) reveals higher proportions of CC and C = O peaks in GQDs-96/HC3N4 compared to C3N4 alone, indicating the successful loading of GQDs-96 onto the surface.

For the N1 s spectra (Fig. 6e), both can be fitted to the three peaks of N-(C)2, N-(C)3, and NH, and the positions of the binding energies of the N-(C)2 and N-(C)3 peaks of C3N4 were shifted from 398.3 eV and 399.7 eV to 398.1 and 399.2 eV for GQDs-96/HC3N4, respectively, which can be ascribed to the fact that after the interfacial interaction between the GQDs and the C3N4 took place interfacial interactions and reactions[40]. Furthermore, a reduction in the NH peak area share for GQDs-96/HC3N4 relative to C3N4 hints at the interaction between NH groups on C3N4 and C = O on GQDs-96.For the O1 s (Fig. 6f) spectra, C3N4 only has one CO peak located near 531.5 eV, whereas GQDs-96/HC3N4 exhibited CO and C = O peaks, and the additional C = O group peak of GQDs-96/HC3N4 may be related to the abundance of carboxyl and carbon groups on the surface of GQDs-96 [41].

These characterizations collectively verify the strong interfacial bonding between GQDs-96 and C3N4. TEM imagery concretely illustrates the successful construction of a well-bonded GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction catalyst.

The capability of the catalyst to decompose the TC and MO under visible light was utilized to assess photocatalytic performance (see Fig. 7a ). Without any catalyst, TC and MO displayed stability, indicating negligible self-degradation. Unmodified C3N4 showed minimal adsorption and catalytic degradation of MO, attributed to its restricted specific surface area, limited visible light absorption, and fast electron-hole recombination rate. The introduction of HC3N4 marginally improved degradation due to its increased surface area from a rod-like structure enhancing photocatalytic activity. The incorporation of CNTs with HC3N4 slightly increased the light absorption capacity and carrier transport, thereby enhancing the degradation efficiency. However, the poor surface functionality and strong van der Waals forces of CNTs led to aggregation, hindering effective interfacial bonding with HC3N4 and limiting photocatalytic improvement. Thus, CNTs/HC3N4 did not exhibit a marked increase in photocatalytic activity for MO degradation.

In contrast, GQDs exhibited markedly enhanced photocatalytic activities. Sequential improvements were observed with GQDs-24/HC3N4, GQDs-48/HC3N4, GQDs-72/HC3N4, and particularly with GQDs-96/HC3N4, which degraded 98 % of MO within 90 min, showcasing superior activity. This suggests that smaller GQD sizes and increased oxygen-containing functional groups contribute to improved photocatalytic efficiency. As shown in Fig. 7b, the degradation kinetics of all catalysts followed the first-order kinetic equation ln (C0/Ct) = kt (k is the reaction rate constant). Compared to C3N4, HC3N4, CNTs/H- C3N4, GQDs-24/HC3N4, GQDs-48/HC3N4, and GQDs-72/HC3N4, the photocatalytic degradation rate of MO by GQDs-96/HC3N4 was increased by 106.10, 11.45, 6.54, 2.36, 1.81, and 1.40 times, respectively. Fig.7c demonstrates a comparison of the photocatalytic removal of MO at 120 min of the photocatalytic reaction with three repetitions of the test for each catalyst.

The performance of the catalysts was further evaluated and the applications of the catalysts were extended by degrading TC under visible light exposure. As illustrated in Fig. 7d, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency for TC showed a similar trend to MO degradation. GQDs-96/HC3N4 exhibited superior photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic degradation rate (Fig. 7e) of GQDs-96/HC3N4 for TC was increased by a factor of 8.4, 4.8, 3.0, 1.6, 1.4, and 1.2 compared to C3N4, HC3N4, CNTs/HC3N4, GQDs-24/HC3N4, GQDs-48/HC3N4, and GQDs-72/HC3N4, respectively. Fig.7f demonstrates a comparison of the photocatalytic removal of TC at 90 min of the photocatalytic reaction with three repetitions of the test for each catalyst.

The durability of GQDs-96/HC3N4 was assessed over multiple MO degradation cycles, maintaining over 80 % efficiency after five cycles, indicating only a 15 % performance decrease (Fig. S4). Further analysis via FT-IR and XRD (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6) confirmed no significant structural or surface changes in GQDs-96/HC3N4 post-degradation cycles, highlighting its excellent cyclic stability [42,43].
The photovoltaic properties and photocatalytic performance enhancement mechanisms were elucidated through UV–vis DRS spectra, PL spectra, and other characterizations.

Fig. 8a presents the absorption edges for each catalyst, revealing that the pristine C3N4 exhibits an absorption edge at 462 nm. This edge blue-shifts to 441 nm for hydrothermally treated HC3N4 and further adjusts after CNTs and GQDs loading. The initially limited absorption of C3N4 and HC3N4 restricts their photocatalytic efficiency, contrasting with the progressive enhancement in light absorption by CNTs/HC3N4 and various GQDs-modified catalysts, extending into the near-infrared region. This improvement correlates with increased electron-hole pair generation, attributed to stronger light absorption [44]. Notably, among all catalysts, GQDs-96/HC3N4 exhibits the strongest light absorption in the visible to near-infrared light region. Especially for the near-infrared region, the absorption curve of GQDs-96/HC3N4 continues to show an upward trend, demonstrating its highly efficient broad-spectrum absorption characteristics. GQDs-96 could realize the perfect combination with HC3N4 because of its smallest particle size and the richest surface functional groups, and its good dispersibility made it uniformly dispersed on the surface of HC3N4. The unique up-conversion effect and surface plasmonic resonance effect of GQDs can ultimately promote the absorption and utilization of low-energy visible light and near-infrared light [45], which is one of the important factors for its efficient photocatalytic performance.

The Tauc plot method (Fig. 8b) [46] indicated that the band gaps of C3N4, HC3N4, and GQDs-96/HC3N4 are 2.82 eV, 2.99 eV, and 2.92 eV, respectively. These values, alongside the observed shifts in absorption edges, suggest GQDs' role in band gap modulation [47]. XPS valence band spectra provided valence band potentials for C3N4 and GQDs-96/HC3N4 at 1.92 eV and 2.21 eV, respectively, which can be further calculated to obtain the conduction band potentials of −0.90 and −0.71 eV for C3N4 and GQDs-96/HC3N4, respectively.

Finally, the energy band of the catalysts was obtained structure (Fig. 8d). The conduction band potentials of C3N4 (−0.90 eV) and GQDs-96/HC3N4 (−0.71 eV) are much more negative than the potential required (−0.33 eV) to generate superoxide radicals (•O2 −), which suggests that both C3N4 and GQDs-96/HC3N4 have sufficient conduction band potentials for generating •O2 −. However, the required valence band potential to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH) is 1.99 eV [48], and the valence band potential of C3N4 (1.92 eV) is difficult to generate enough •OH [49], whereas that of GQDs-96/HC3N4 (2.21 eV) is more positive than that required to generate •OH.

The migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers was scrutinized using PL spectroscopy. Fig. 8e shows that pristine C3N4 exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity, indicating a high rate of electron-hole recombination and low carrier migration efficiency. Conversely, GQDs-96/HC3N4 demonstrates significantly lower fluorescence intensity, suggesting superior migration efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Because GQDs-96 has the smallest particle size, the richest surface functional groups, and the best dispersion stability, it could achieve the strongest interfacial bonding with HC3N4 and the most homogeneous interfacial distribution compared with other GQDs during the hydrothermal construction of heterojunction catalysts. These uniformly distributed GQDs-96 act as carriers for the fast electron transport channels, and ultimately, when the photocatalyst is excited by light, the electrons and holes can migrate to the surface of the catalyst as much as possible to engage in photocatalytic redox reactions [50,51]. which effectively avoids the recombination of the electrons and holes leading to the release of fluorescence, and thus the GQDs-96/HC3N4 has the lowest fluorescence intensity. In addition, the shift in emission wavelength across the catalysts aligns with the absorption edge changes observed in UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, underscoring the correlation between light absorption and carrier migration efficiency.

TPR spectroscopy further revealed the photocurrent response of catalysts under visible light (see Fig. 8f), with GQDs-96/HC3N4 exhibiting a photocurrent intensity five times that of initial C3N4. This confirms the exceptional charge separation and transfer capabilities of GQDs-96/HC3N4, highlighting its enhanced responsiveness to visible light [52].

The predominant reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation of MO by GQDs-96/HC3N4 were identified by a trapping agent assay. Trapping agents p-benzoquinone (PBQ), isopropanol (IPQ), and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were employed to target superoxide radical (•O2 −), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and photogenerated holes (h +), respectively. As shown in Fig. 9a , the degradation efficiencies of GQDs-96/HC3N4 on MO at 90 min were 98 %, 30 %, 63 %, and 38 % in the absence and presence of PBQ, IPQ, and Na2C2O4, respectively. These findings reveal that the magnitude of the effect of reactive substances by GQDs-96/HC3N4 follows the order: •O2 − > h + >•OH on MO degradation.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) tests on C3N4 and GQDs-96/HC3N4 under light conditions aimed to quantify the generation of active substances. The h + produced by the catalyst under light can oxidize TEMPO to produce TEMPO+, where TEMPO has a strong ESR signal, while its oxidation product TEMPO+ has a weak ESR signal [53].

Under dark conditions, both catalysts showed strong h + signals, with C3N4's signal slightly decreasing upon light exposure, indicating limited h + production (see Fig. 9b). In stark contrast, GQDs-96/HC3N4's h + signal significantly diminished after light exposure, evidencing abundant h + generation and efficient surface migration for participation in redox reactions. Furthermore, the emergence of stronger •O2 − and •OH (see Fig. 9c and d) signal peaks under light exposure for GQDs-96/HC3N4 compared to C3N4 suggests superior production of these reactive species, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance.

The mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity of GQDs-96/HC3N4 is presented in Fig. 10 . The initial C3N4 utilizes the visible light weakly (<460 nm). Moreover, limited specific surface area (few active sites for photocatalytic reactions) and poor electrical conductivity of bulk C3N4 resulted in poor photocatalytic [54]. After the construction of GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction by hydrothermal method, the energy band structure of the catalyst was adjusted to be able to satisfy produce •OH and •O2 −. GQDs-96 enhance visible and near-infrared light absorption, providing more energy for electron-hole pair generation. Leveraging the excellent conductivity of graphene-based materials, GQDs-96 act as carriers for photo-induced electrons, reducing recombination rates, and accelerating electron migration. This leads to an increased surface migration of electrons and holes, enhancing redox reaction participation. Consequently, the GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction catalyzes the degradation of pollutants into non-toxic products through a synergistic action of •O2 −, h +, and •OH [55].

The GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction, constructed by the GQDs-96 with the smallest particle size and the richest surface functional groups, exhibits superior photocatalytic activity. This superiority is attributed to several key factors:
1.
The zeta potential measurements indicate excellent dispersion stability of GQDs-96, ensuring uniform dispersion on the C3N4 substrate during the hydrothermal process. This uniformity is crucial for expanding the photocatalytic interface area, directly impacting the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions.
2.
The smaller nanoparticle size allows for their optimal distribution within the pores and interlayers of the C3N4 matrix. Such distribution not only optimizes the use of available surface area but also significantly shortens the transport distance for electrons. This reduction in transport distance enhances electron storage and transfer mechanisms, critical for effective photocatalysis.
3.
At the nanoscale, GQDs exhibit a quantum confinement effect, intensifying their inherent redox potential. This phenomenon plays a vital role in bolstering the photocatalytic redox reactions under light irradiation, leading to an increase in the degradation rate of pollutants.
4.
The rich presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, particularly carbonyl groups (C=O), on GQDs-96 enhances their ability to form strong interfacial bonds with the amino groups (NH) present on the C3N4 surface. Such strong bonding is instrumental in facilitating efficient charge transfer between GQDs-96 and C3N4, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance.

In all, the meticulously engineered GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction showcases superior photocatalytic performance due to its enhanced light absorption, reduced electron-hole recombination, and improved charge carrier mobility [56]. These attributes ensure that GQDs-96/HC3N4 not only efficiently generates reactive oxidative species but also maximizes their utilization in the degradation of pollutants.

In this study, GQDs of varying particle sizes and surface functionalities (GQDs-24, GQDs-48, GQDs-72, and GQDs-96) were synthesized through controlled reaction times with concentrated HNO3 and CNTs. Among these, GQDs-96, characterized by the smallest particle size and a rich array of functional groups, demonstrated exceptional capability for strong interfacial bonding and uniform distribution alongside HC3N4. This specific iteration of GQDs significantly improved visible light absorption and served as an efficient electron migration pathway, thereby facilitating the surface migration of electrons and holes for enhanced redox reaction participation.

Compared to C3N4, the GQDs-96/HC3N4 heterojunction displayed a remarkable ability to generate higher yields of h +, •O2 − , and •OH by enhancing the interface area and promoting effective charge carrier dynamics. This culminated in a highly efficient photocatalytic performance, particularly in the degradation of TC and MO. The outcomes of this research not only provide a novel strategy for designing the particle size and surface chemistry of GQDs to optimize the photocatalytic efficiency of C3N4 but also open new avenues for leveraging these insights in environmental remediation and energy conversion applications.

However, the experimentally prepared GQDs in this paper exhibited low yields, which limits the further industrialization of GQDs for applications, so there is an urgent need to develop a low-cost and high-volume (kilogram-scale) preparation technique for GQDs to further expand the photovoltaic applications of GQDs.

Youliang Nie: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rui Bao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Xiudi Xiao: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jianhong Yi: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gang Xu: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Chinese National Science Foundation (Grant No. 52064032, 52174345), the Yunnan Science and Technology projects (Grant No. 202202AG050004), Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Plan Project (Grant No. 2022RC4019).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.105343.
